QF.id: Taxation of Debt and Equity Products_7

question.text: Under current tax law, which of the following instruments allows the possibility of long-term capital gains treatment?

answer.text: Market Linked Debentures (MLDs) held for more than 12 months
Unlisted bonds held for more than 24 months
Listed government securities held for more than 12 months
Specified mutual funds (≥65% debt) purchased after 1st April 2023 and held for more than 24 months

explanation.text: **The Logic:** Listed government securities (G-Secs in bond form) follow the same rules as listed bonds/debentures — they qualify for LTCG after a 12-month holding period, taxed at 12.5% without indexation. The other three options all fall under Section 50AA, which mandates ALWAYS short-term treatment regardless of holding period: MLDs (always STCG), unlisted bonds (always STCG), and specified MFs with ≥65% debt purchased after 1-4-2023 (always STCG). **The Trap:** G-Secs might seem like they should fall under the same restrictive debt rules, but when issued in bond form and listed, they follow listed bond taxation. Candidates who over-apply Section 50AA to all debt instruments will get this wrong. **Cross-Exam Context:** NISM Series IV (Interest Rate Derivatives) covers G-Sec characteristics; the taxation angle is tested in Series X-B. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** Listed G-Sec bonds are among the few remaining debt instruments that offer LTCG at 12.5%. For tax-conscious investors seeking debt exposure, this makes them relatively attractive post the Section 50AA changes.
